content.full_text:
the reviewing body may amend
codes on its own initiative

State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 N STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • • Administration • • • Executive level • • • Conflict of Interest
(916) 322-5662        322-5662        322-5601        222-6441        322-6444

December 21, 1978

A-98-338

William J. Schwartz, Jr.
Office of County Counsel
County of San Diego
355 County Administration Center
San Diego, CA 92101

Dear Bill:

Thank you for your letter of October 31, 1978 requesting
advice with respect to the conflict of interest provisions of
the Political Reform Act.

You have asked whether the Board of Supervisors on its own
initiative may propose and act upon amendments to codes for
which it is the code reviewing body without first having each
agency propose such amendments.

Generally speaking, the answer to this question is yes so
long as the employees of each affected agency have an opportunity
to state their views on the proposed amendments. If the effect
of the proposed amendments is to conform the code to the definition
contained in the Act or regulations adopted by the Commission,
you may be able to accomplish such changes without observing
the cumbersome notice and hearing procedures depending on the
requirements of your local ordinance.

If you have any further questions, please do not hesitate
to contact me.

Sincerely,

Ted Prim. Chief
Conflicts of Interest Division

TP:nrw

County of San Diego
OFFICE OF
COUNTY COUNSEL
355 COUNTY ADMINISTRATION CENTER
SAN DIEGO, CALIFORNIA 92101
(714) 236-3651

DONALD L. CLARK
County Counsel

JOSEPH KASE, JR.
Assistant County Counsel

October 31, 1978

Ted Prim, Esq.
Staff Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California 95814

Dear Mr. Prim:

Subsequent to approval of Conflict of Interest Codes by
the San Diego County Board of Supervisors as Code Reviewing
Body, the California legislature has made statutory changes
in Government Code section 8100, et seq., amending the
Political Reform Act of 1974. Conflict of Interest Codes
incorporate the provisions of the Political Reform Act,
state regulations and other County requirements pertaining
to conflict of interest. It appears to us that amendment to
the Codes would be appropriate to bring them into conformance
with the new state legislation.

Government Code Section 87307 does not specifically
authorize the Board of Supervisors as the Code Reviewing
Body to initiate changes such as these. However, it appears
to be a reasonable and logical extension of its powers to
have this authority. Please advise us as to whether it
would be proper procedure in making wholesale changes to all
Codes for the Board of Supervisors on its own initiative to
propose and act on amendments without undergoing the cumbersome
process of having each agency propose amendments.

Very truly yours,

DONALD L. CLARK, County Counsel

By [signature]
WILLIAM J. SCHWARTZ, JR., Deputy

WJS/EB/cac

cc Clerk, Board of Supervisors

sections.question: You have asked whether the Board of Supervisors on its own initiative may propose and act upon amendments to codes for which it is the code reviewing body without first having each agency propose such amendments.
sections.conclusion: Generally speaking, the answer to this question is yes so long as the employees of each affected agency have an opportunity to state their views on the proposed amendments. If the effect of the proposed amendments is to conform the code to the definition contained in the Act or regulations adopted by the Commission, you may be able to accomplish such changes without observing the cumbersome notice and hearing procedures depending on the requirements of your local ordinance.
sections.question_synthetic: Can the Board of Supervisors, in its capacity as code reviewing body, initiate and approve amendments to conflict of interest codes on its own initiative, or must each affected agency propose amendments first?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Board of Supervisors may initiate code amendments without agency proposals, provided affected agency employees have an opportunity to comment. If amendments merely conform codes to the Political Reform Act or FPPC regulations, simplified notice and hearing procedures may apply.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both the incoming request (October 31, 1978) and the FPPC response letter (December 21, 1978). Extracted as advice_letter with is_fppc_response=true because the primary document is the FPPC's response.

CONCLUSION: Generally speaking, the answer to this question is yes so long as the employees of each affected agency have an opportunity to state their views on the proposed amendments. If the effect of the proposed amendments is to conform the code to the definition contained in the Act or regulations adopted by the Commission, you may be able to accomplish such changes without observing the cumbersome notice and hearing procedures depending on the requirements of your local ordinance.

embedding.summary: San Diego County Counsel requested advice on whether the Board of Supervisors may initiate amendments to conflict of interest codes to conform them to new state legislation. The FPPC Chief of the Conflicts of Interest Division advised that this is permissible if affected employees have an opportunity to comment and, for conforming amendments, simplified procedures may apply.
